title: Introduction to Data Visualization in R
text:
Workshop materials and instructions for the Introduction to Data Visualization in R virtual workshop, led by JHU Data Services.
This workshop introduces data visualization in R using base R and ggplot2. In this hands-on workshop, we cover design concepts of data visualization and popular R packages, before diving into creating data visualizations for a prepared dataset using base R and ggplot2.


If you have taken the live webinar for this class, please take this survey: Intro to Data Vis in R Survey






Presentation Slides (PDF)





Base R
ggplot2





Base Graphics Cheatsheet
ggplot2 Cheatsheet





Website: dataservices.library.jhu.edu Contact us: dataservices@jhu.edu
JHU Data Services, part of the Johns Hopkins University Sheridan Libraries, helps the JHU community find, use, visualize, manage, and share data. We offer live webinars and self-paced online trainings on computational research and coding, GIS, data management, data visualization, and more. See all of our training topics on our website.



The presentation materials are licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International License (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0), attributable to Data Services, Johns Hopkins University.
See LICENSE file for additional code licensing and re-use information.
The images, external resources, and cheatsheets linked in this repository may have other licenses and terms of use.



Please cite this material as: Johns Hopkins University Data Services. March 4, 2026. Introduction to Data Visualization in R

title: Introduction to Data Visualization in R
text: Please cite this material as: Johns Hopkins University Data Services. March 4, 2026. Introduction to Data Visualization in R

title: Lesson 1 - Base R
text:
We will be using the Palmer Penguins dataset for today’s visualization exercises.



Palmer Penguin Illustration


Data were collected and made available by Dr. Kristen Gorman and the Palmer Station, Antarctica LTER, a member of the Long Term Ecological Research Network. The data were conveniently collected as an R package that we will install (if you haven’t already) and use today.



Let’s load in the Palmer penguin data, by importing the palmerpenguins package. If you have not already installed this package, you can do so now by running install.packages('palmerpenguins') in your R console.

# load in dataset
library(palmerpenguins)


Attaching package: 'palmerpenguins'


The following objects are masked from 'package:datasets':

    penguins, penguins_raw

data(package = "palmerpenguins")





Let’s explore the contents of the data. We can use the head command to look at the first few rows of the data.

head(x = penguins)

# A tibble: 6 × 8
  species island    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm body_mass_g
  <fct>   <fct>              <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>       <int>
1 Adelie  Torgersen           39.1          18.7               181        3750
2 Adelie  Torgersen           39.5          17.4               186        3800
3 Adelie  Torgersen           40.3          18                 195        3250
4 Adelie  Torgersen           NA            NA                  NA          NA
5 Adelie  Torgersen           36.7          19.3               193        3450
6 Adelie  Torgersen           39.3          20.6               190        3650
# ℹ 2 more variables: sex <fct>, year <int>







Visualizing one variable is a useful way to understand the shape of the distribution of the variable in question.



Let’s begin with a histogram. We want to visualize the frequency that values in certain ranges occur in our data.

hist(x = penguins$bill_length_mm)










In descriptive statistics, a box plot (or box and whisker plot) is a method for visualizing a simple summary of the distribution of a continuous variable. A boxplot shows the distribution of data using five summary values: the “minimum”, the first (or lower) quartile, the median, the third (or upper) quartile, and the “maximum”.
The middle line of the box is the median value. The top and bottom of the box are the “hinges” and corresponds to the lower and upper quartiles respectively. The whiskers extending outward are sometimes called the minimum and maximum, which introduces some confusion. They are actually the minimum and maximum values that do not exceed a certain distance from the median. That distance is 1.5 x the IQR. If no value exceeds that distance, than the whiskers are the minimum and maximum values.



Boxplot Element
Description




Interquartile Range (IQR)
Difference between Q3 and Q1


Maximum
\(Q3 + (1.5*IQR)\)


Minimum
\(Q1 - (1.5*IQR)\)


Q1
The median of the lower half of the dataset


Median
The median of the complete dataset.


Q3
The median of the upper half of the dataset








Boxplots provide a more concise representation of our data. Let’s look at a boxplot of bill lengths to better understand how bill lengths are distributed among the penguins studied.

boxplot(x = penguins$bill_length_mm)








That only provides us information for the entire population. Let’s look at a boxplot of bill lengths by species to better understand how bill lengths are distributed among individual species of the penguins studied.

boxplot(formula = penguins$bill_length_mm ~ penguins$species)








We don’t need to explicitly refer to the dataframes columns if we provide the data as an argument

boxplot(formula = bill_length_mm ~ species, data = penguins)








We can improve this visualization by removing non-encoding borders, and adding axis labels

boxplot(formula = bill_length_mm ~ species, 
        data = penguins,
        frame = FALSE, 
        xlab = "Species",
        ylab = "Bill Length (mm)")












Visualizing two variables allows you to study the relationship between them. Lets see how the penguin’s bill length changes with respect to body mass.

plot(x = penguins$body_mass_g, 
     y = penguins$bill_length_mm,
     xlab = "Body Mass (g)",
     ylab = "Bill Length (mm)") 











What does this plot tell you about the relationship between penguin bill length and body mass?
Do you notice anything unusual in this visualization?


There appears to be a correlation between body mass and bill length. There appears to be an overall linear trend, but there appears to be some outliers in the upper left portion of the plot. Perhaps we can elucidate what is happening here by plotting more information. Let’s color by species to see how species impacts this relationship.

plot(x = penguins$body_mass_g, 
     y = penguins$bill_length_mm,
     col = penguins$species)








Now we don’t know what these colors mean, we need to add a legend:

plot(x = penguins$body_mass_g, 
     y = penguins$bill_length_mm,
     col = penguins$species,
     xlab = "Body Mass (g)",
     ylab = "Bill Length (mm)") 
legend(x = "bottomright", 
       legend = unique(penguins$species),
       col = unique(penguins$species),
       pch = 21)











Let’s begin by giving the plot meaningful axis titles using the xlab and ylab arguments for the x and y axes respectively.

plot(x = penguins$body_mass_g, 
     y = penguins$bill_length_mm,
     col = penguins$species,
     xlab = "Body Mass (g)",
     ylab = "Bill Length (mm)")
legend(x = "bottomright", 
       legend = unique(penguins$species),
       col = unique(penguins$species),
       pch = 21)








Now let’s add a title using the main argument.

plot(x = penguins$body_mass_g, 
     y = penguins$bill_length_mm,
     col = penguins$species,
     xlab = "Body Mass (g)",
     ylab = "Bill Length (mm)",
     main = "Bill length and body mass")
legend(x = "bottomright", 
       legend = unique(penguins$species),
       col = unique(penguins$species),
       pch = 21)








Lets make the scatter points more visible using pch (plotting character), which controls the geometric shape used to represent our data. In this case, let’s switch from the open circle to the solid circle.

plot(x = penguins$body_mass_g, 
     y = penguins$bill_length_mm,
     col = penguins$species,
     xlab = "Body Mass (g)",
     ylab = "Bill Length (mm)",
     main = "Bill length and body mass",
     pch = 16)
legend(x = "bottomright", 
       legend = unique(penguins$species),
       col = unique(penguins$species),
       pch = 16)








Now let’s increase the size of our points using the cex argument. cex controls the amount by which plotted symbols should be scaled. 1 is the default, 1.5 is 50% larger, .5 is 50% smaller, etc.

plot(x = penguins$body_mass_g, 
     y = penguins$bill_length_mm,
     col = penguins$species,
     xlab = "Body Mass (g)",
     ylab = "Bill Length (mm)",
     main = "Bill length and body mass",
     pch = 16,
     cex = 1.2)
legend(x = "bottomright", 
       legend = unique(penguins$species),
       col = unique(penguins$species),
       pch = 16,
       cex = 1.2)










Can you identify any problems with the default color scheme?

Let’s use a colorblind friendly color palette. We will use the RColorBrewer package to generate a colorblind friendly palette.

display.brewer.all(colorblindFriendly = TRUE)









# let's "brew" a colorblind friendly color palette using rcolorbrewer
colorblind_palette <- brewer.pal(n = 3, name = "Dark2")

palette(value = colorblind_palette)

plot(x = penguins$body_mass_g,
     y = penguins$bill_length_mm,
     col = penguins$species,
     xlab = "Body Mass (g)",
     ylab = "Bill Length (mm)",
     main = "Bill length and body mass",
     pch = 16,
     cex = 1.2)
legend(x = "bottomright", 
       legend = unique(penguins$species),
       col = unique(penguins$species),
       pch = 16,
       cex = 1.2)








Now let’s add a grid:

# let's "brew" a colorblind friendly color palette using rcolorbrewer
colorblind_palette <- brewer.pal(n = 3, name = "Dark2")

palette(value = colorblind_palette)

plot(x = penguins$body_mass_g,
     y = penguins$bill_length_mm,
     col = penguins$species,
     xlab = "Body Mass (g)",
     ylab = "Bill Length (mm)",
     main = "Bill length and body mass",
     pch = 16,
     cex = 1.2)
grid()
legend(x = "bottomright", 
       legend = unique(penguins$species),
       col = unique(penguins$species),
       pch = 16,
       cex = 1.2)








Now let’s remove unnecessary borders using the bty argument.

# let's "brew" a colorblind friendly color palette using rcolorbrewer
colorblind_palette <- brewer.pal(n = 3, name = "Dark2")

palette(value = colorblind_palette)

plot(x = penguins$body_mass_g,
     y = penguins$bill_length_mm,
     col = penguins$species,
     xlab = "Body Mass (g)",
     ylab = "Bill Length (mm)",
     main = "Bill length and body mass",
     pch = 16,
     cex = 1.2, 
     bty = "l")
grid()
legend(x = "bottomright", 
       legend = unique(penguins$species),
       col = unique(penguins$species),
       pch = 16,
       cex = 1.2)








Now let’s increase the size of our labels.

# let's "brew" a colorblind friendly color palette using rcolorbrewer
colorblind_palette <- brewer.pal(n = 3, name = "Dark2")

palette(value = colorblind_palette)

plot(x = penguins$body_mass_g,
     y = penguins$bill_length_mm,
     col = penguins$species,
     xlab = "Body Mass (g)",
     ylab = "Bill Length (mm)",
     main = "Bill length and body mass",
     pch = 16,
     cex = 1.2, 
     bty = "l",
     cex.axis = 1.2,
     cex.lab = 1.3,
     cex.main = 1.5)
grid()
legend(x = "bottomright", 
       legend = unique(penguins$species),
       col = unique(penguins$species),
       pch = 16,
       cex = 1.2)








Finally, let’s left justify our plot tile and add a subtitle. We will use the margin text (mtext) command to annotate our plot in the margins. This is the easiest way to place a title and subtitle with fine control over its positioning.

# let's "brew" a colorblind friendly color palette using rcolorbrewer
colorblind_palette <- brewer.pal(n = 3, name = "Dark2")

palette(value = colorblind_palette)

plot(x = penguins$body_mass_g,
     y = penguins$bill_length_mm,
     col = penguins$species,
     xlab = "Body Mass (g)",
     ylab = "Bill Length",
     pch = 16,
     cex = 1.2, 
     bty = "l",
     cex.axis = 1.2,
     cex.lab = 1.3,
     cex.main = 1.5)
mtext(
  text = "Bill length and body mass",
  side = 3,
  adj = 0,
  line = 2,
  cex = 1.4,
  font = 2
)

mtext(
  text = "Measurements for Adelie, Chinstrap and Gentoo Penguins at Palmer Station LTER",
  side = 3,
  adj = 0,
  line = 1,
  cex = .9,
  font = 2
)

grid()
legend(x = "bottomright", 
       legend = unique(penguins$species),
       col = unique(penguins$species),
       pch = 16,
       cex = 1.2,
       bty = "n")








Now, let’s save the resulting publication ready figure:

# let's "brew" a colorblind friendly color palette using rcolorbrewer
color_friendly_palette  <- brewer.pal(n = 3, name = "Dark2")

pdf(file = "penguin_mass_comparison.pdf", 
    height = 5,
    width = 8)
plot(x = penguins$body_mass_g,
     y = penguins$bill_length_mm,
     col = penguins$species,
     xlab = "Body Mass (g)",
     ylab = "Bill Length",
     pch = 16,
     cex = 1.2, 
     bty = "l",
     cex.axis = 1.2,
     cex.lab = 1.3,
     cex.main = 1.5)
mtext(
  text = "Bill length and body mass",
  side = 3,
  adj = 0,
  line = 2,
  cex = 1.4,
  font = 2
)

mtext(
  text = "Measurements for Adelie, Chinstrap and Gentoo Penguins at Palmer Station LTER",
  side = 3,
  adj = 0,
  line = 1,
  cex = .9,
  font = 2
)

grid()
legend(x = "bottomright", 
       legend = unique(penguins$species),
       col = unique(penguins$species),
       pch = 16,
       cex = 1.2,
       bty = "n")
dev.off()

quartz_off_screen 
                2

title: Lesson 1 - Base R
text:
We will be using the Palmer Penguins dataset for today’s visualization exercises.



Palmer Penguin Illustration


Data were collected and made available by Dr. Kristen Gorman and the Palmer Station, Antarctica LTER, a member of the Long Term Ecological Research Network. The data were conveniently collected as an R package that we will install (if you haven’t already) and use today.

library(palmerpenguins)


Attaching package: 'palmerpenguins'


The following objects are masked from 'package:datasets':

    penguins, penguins_raw

head(x = penguins)

  species island    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm body_mass_g
  <fct>   <fct>              <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>       <int>
1 Adelie  Torgersen           39.1          18.7               181        3750
2 Adelie  Torgersen           39.5          17.4               186        3800
3 Adelie  Torgersen           40.3          18                 195        3250

6 Adelie  Torgersen           39.3          20.6               190        3650
# ℹ 2 more variables: sex <fct>, year <int>

hist(x = penguins$bill_length_mm)

Description




Interquartile Range (IQR)
Difference between Q3 and Q1


Maximum
\(Q3 + (1.5*IQR)\)


Minimum
\(Q1 - (1.5*IQR)\)


Q1
The median of the lower half of the dataset

The median of the complete dataset.


Q3
The median of the upper half of the dataset

       legend = unique(penguins$species),
       col = unique(penguins$species),
       pch = 21)

plot(x = penguins$body_mass_g, 
     y = penguins$bill_length_mm,

     ylab = "Bill Length (mm)")

       legend = unique(penguins$species),
       col = unique(penguins$species),
       pch = 21)

plot(x = penguins$body_mass_g, 
     y = penguins$bill_length_mm,
     col = penguins$species,

     ylab = "Bill Length (mm)",
     main = "Bill length and body mass",
     pch = 16)

       legend = unique(penguins$species),
       col = unique(penguins$species),
       pch = 16)

plot(x = penguins$body_mass_g,

     main = "Bill length and body mass",
     pch = 16,
     cex = 1.2)
legend(x = "bottomright", 
       legend = unique(penguins$species),
       col = unique(penguins$species),
       pch = 16,
       cex = 1.2)

display.brewer.all(colorblindFriendly = TRUE)









# let's "brew" a colorblind friendly color palette using rcolorbrewer
colorblind_palette <- brewer.pal(n = 3, name = "Dark2")

palette(value = colorblind_palette)

plot(x = penguins$body_mass_g,
     y = penguins$bill_length_mm,
     col = penguins$species,
     xlab = "Body Mass (g)",
     ylab = "Bill Length (mm)",
     main = "Bill length and body mass",
     pch = 16,
     cex = 1.2)
legend(x = "bottomright", 
       legend = unique(penguins$species),
       col = unique(penguins$species),
       pch = 16,
       cex = 1.2)








Now let’s add a grid:

# let's "brew" a colorblind friendly color palette using rcolorbrewer
colorblind_palette <- brewer.pal(n = 3, name = "Dark2")

palette(value = colorblind_palette)

plot(x = penguins$body_mass_g,
     y = penguins$bill_length_mm,
     col = penguins$species,
     xlab = "Body Mass (g)",
     ylab = "Bill Length (mm)",
     main = "Bill length and body mass",
     pch = 16,
     cex = 1.2)
grid()
legend(x = "bottomright", 
       legend = unique(penguins$species),
       col = unique(penguins$species),
       pch = 16,
       cex = 1.2)








Now let’s remove unnecessary borders using the bty argument.

# let's "brew" a colorblind friendly color palette using rcolorbrewer
colorblind_palette <- brewer.pal(n = 3, name = "Dark2")

palette(value = colorblind_palette)

plot(x = penguins$body_mass_g,
     y = penguins$bill_length_mm,
     col = penguins$species,
     xlab = "Body Mass (g)",
     ylab = "Bill Length (mm)",
     main = "Bill length and body mass",
     pch = 16,
     cex = 1.2, 
     bty = "l")

legend(x = "bottomright", 
       legend = unique(penguins$species),
       col = unique(penguins$species),
       pch = 16,
       cex = 1.2)

# let's "brew" a colorblind friendly color palette using rcolorbrewer

     y = penguins$bill_length_mm,
     col = penguins$species,
     xlab = "Body Mass (g)",
     ylab = "Bill Length (mm)",
     main = "Bill length and body mass",
     pch = 16,
     cex = 1.2, 
     bty = "l",
     cex.axis = 1.2,
     cex.lab = 1.3,
     cex.main = 1.5)
grid()
legend(x = "bottomright", 
       legend = unique(penguins$species),
       col = unique(penguins$species),
       pch = 16,
       cex = 1.2)

# let's "brew" a colorblind friendly color palette using rcolorbrewer
colorblind_palette <- brewer.pal(n = 3, name = "Dark2")

palette(value = colorblind_palette)

plot(x = penguins$body_mass_g,
     y = penguins$bill_length_mm,
     col = penguins$species,
     xlab = "Body Mass (g)",
     ylab = "Bill Length",
     pch = 16,
     cex = 1.2, 
     bty = "l",
     cex.axis = 1.2,
     cex.lab = 1.3,
     cex.main = 1.5)
mtext(
  text = "Bill length and body mass",
  side = 3,
  adj = 0,
  line = 2,
  cex = 1.4,
  font = 2
)

mtext(
  text = "Measurements for Adelie, Chinstrap and Gentoo Penguins at Palmer Station LTER",
  side = 3,
  adj = 0,
  line = 1,
  cex = .9,
  font = 2

grid()
legend(x = "bottomright", 
       legend = unique(penguins$species),
       col = unique(penguins$species),
       pch = 16,
       cex = 1.2,
       bty = "n")

# let's "brew" a colorblind friendly color palette using rcolorbrewer
color_friendly_palette  <- brewer.pal(n = 3, name = "Dark2")

pdf(file = "penguin_mass_comparison.pdf", 
    height = 5,
    width = 8)
plot(x = penguins$body_mass_g,
     y = penguins$bill_length_mm,
     col = penguins$species,
     xlab = "Body Mass (g)",
     ylab = "Bill Length",
     pch = 16,
     cex = 1.2, 
     bty = "l",
     cex.axis = 1.2,
     cex.lab = 1.3,
     cex.main = 1.5)
mtext(
  text = "Bill length and body mass",
  side = 3,
  adj = 0,
  line = 2,
  cex = 1.4,
  font = 2
)

mtext(
  text = "Measurements for Adelie, Chinstrap and Gentoo Penguins at Palmer Station LTER",
  side = 3,
  adj = 0,
  line = 1,
  cex = .9,
  font = 2
)

grid()
legend(x = "bottomright", 
       legend = unique(penguins$species),
       col = unique(penguins$species),
       pch = 16,
       cex = 1.2,
       bty = "n")
dev.off()

quartz_off_screen 
                2

title: Lesson 2 - ggplot2
text:
ggplot2 is an R package for producing graphics, and it is particularly well suited to producing statistical graphics. ggplot2 is based on the Grammar of Graphics, which describes a series of components that make up graphs. ggplot2 allows you to build graphs out of these independent components, much like Legos. Rather than be limited to a set number of predefined graphs, you can create a vast variety of graphics by adding, removing, and mixing these components.
Today we will learn what these components are, and how we can combine them to create effective graphics in R.


Each ggplot2 plot has three key components:

Data: The dataframe you would like to plot.
Aesthetic mappings between the variables of interest in your data, and a visual property (like size, color, or position)
At least one layer. Layers describe how observations should be rendered (bars, points, etc) and are created with the geom function.

title: Lesson 2 - ggplot2
text:
Each ggplot2 plot has three key components:

Data: The dataframe you would like to plot.
Aesthetic mappings between the variables of interest in your data, and a visual property (like size, color, or position)
At least one layer. Layers describe how observations should be rendered (bars, points, etc) and are created with the geom function.

title: Lesson 2 - ggplot2
text:
Load the libraries and data.

Let’s load in the Palmer penguin data, by importing the palmerpenguins package. If you have not already installed this package, you can do so now by running install.packages('palmerpenguins') in your R console. We will also load our libraries for plotting.

# load libraries
library(ggplot2)
library(scales)
library(RColorBrewer)
library(palmerpenguins)


Attaching package: 'palmerpenguins'


The following objects are masked from 'package:datasets':

    penguins, penguins_raw

# load in dataset
data(package = "palmerpenguins")

title: Lesson 2 - ggplot2
text:
Explore the data

Let’s explore the contents of the data. We can use the head command to look at the first few rows of the data.

head(penguins)

# A tibble: 6 × 8
  species island    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm body_mass_g
  <fct>   <fct>              <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>       <int>
1 Adelie  Torgersen           39.1          18.7               181        3750
2 Adelie  Torgersen           39.5          17.4               186        3800
3 Adelie  Torgersen           40.3          18                 195        3250
4 Adelie  Torgersen           NA            NA                  NA          NA
5 Adelie  Torgersen           36.7          19.3               193        3450
6 Adelie  Torgersen           39.3          20.6               190        3650
# ℹ 2 more variables: sex <fct>, year <int>

title: Lesson 2 - ggplot2
text:
Using ggplot2


Plotting Data

When we create a plot with ggplot2() we do it incrementally, by adding a layer at a time. Lets begin by calling ggplot() and passing our dataset (penguin).

Activity
What happens when we run: ggplot(data = penguins)?


ggplot(data = penguins)








Nothing is produced! This is because we need to add a layer. Let’s add a geom layer, which will specify the geometric object we want to use to draw each observation. Let’s use geom_point() to create a scatter plot. Try to run the code again with geom_point() added and see what happens?

ggplot(data = penguins) +
  geom_point()

We received an error because we haven’t specified what elements of our data we want to plot.
Lets map to variables in our data to aesthetic elements. In this case let’s map body_mass_g to the x-axis, and bill_length_mm to the y-axis.

ggplot(data = penguins) +
  geom_point(aes(x = body_mass_g, 
                 y = bill_length_mm))

Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).









It worked!


Modifying Aesthetics

Now lets configure another aesthetic of geom_point() by specifying a color for the points, using the argument color. Lets use the color "steelblue".

Activity
Add color = "steelblue" as an additional argument to aes() within geom_point
What happens?


ggplot(data = penguins) +
  geom_point(aes(x = body_mass_g, 
                 y = bill_length_mm,
                 color = "steelblue")) 

Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).









The points are colored red, even though we explicitly specified that they be steelblue. This unexpected behaviour is related to how ggplot2 handles aesthetic arguments. Those that are defined within aes() map data to an aesthetic using a scale.
Those that are defined outside of aes() are set to a fixed value. Let’s try again, but move color = "steelblue" outside of aes().

ggplot(data = penguins) +
  geom_point(aes(x = body_mass_g, 
                 y = bill_length_mm),
                 color = "steelblue") 

Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).









That produces the result we expected. Remember, if you want to set an aesthetic to a fixed value (such as setting points to the color steelblue) then do it outside of aes(). If you want to map some variable of your data to an aesthetic automatically, then do it inside aes()
Let’s try mapping the species variable to color:

ggplot(data = penguins) +
  geom_point(aes(x = body_mass_g, 
                 y = bill_length_mm,
                 color = species))

Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).









This produces points colored by species, and automatically creates an appropriately named legend. The individual points look a little small, so let’s increase their size.

ggplot(data = penguins) +
  geom_point(aes(x = body_mass_g, 
                 y = bill_length_mm,
                 color = species),
             size = 3)

Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).









Now let’s add transparency so that we can see points being occluded by other points.

ggplot(data = penguins) +
  geom_point(aes(x = body_mass_g, 
                 y = bill_length_mm,
                 color = species),
             size = 3,
             alpha = .5)

Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).











Using RColorBrewer Palettes

This is looking better, but the default use of red and green is not colorblind friendly. Let’s correct this by using a palette from RColorBrewer. Fortunately, with ggplot2 we can pass the palette directly in to ggplot2:

ggplot(data = penguins) +
  geom_point(aes(x = body_mass_g, 
                 y = bill_length_mm,
                 color = species),
             size = 3,
             alpha = .5) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2")

Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).









Now let’s clean up those axis labels:

ggplot(data = penguins) +
  geom_point(aes(x = body_mass_g, 
                 y = bill_length_mm,
                 color = species),
             size = 3,
             alpha = .5) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2") +
  xlab("Body Mass (g)") +
  ylab("Bill Length (mm)")

Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).











Themes

Remember, never trust the defaults. Although ggplot2 plots look much nicer than base R, that does not mean they are ready for publication. There are a few things we can do to clean this plot up. Let’s start my removing that unnecessary grey background. We can do this easily by applying the minimal theme:

ggplot(data = penguins) +
  geom_point(aes(x = body_mass_g, 
                 y = bill_length_mm,
                 color = species),
             size = 3,
             alpha = .5) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2") +
  xlab("Body Mass (g)") +
  ylab("Bill Length (mm)") +
  theme_minimal()

Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).









Lets increase the size of our x and y label font and axis label font to make the labels more visible. To do this, we will need to manually modify the theme:

ggplot(data = penguins) +
  geom_point(aes(x = body_mass_g, 
                 y = bill_length_mm,
                 color = species),
             size = 3,
             alpha = .5) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2") +
  xlab("Body Mass (g)") +
  ylab("Bill Length (mm)") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.title.x = element_text(size = 14),
        axis.title.y = element_text(size = 14),
        axis.text = element_text(size = 12)) 

Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).










Activity
What happens when you switch the positions of theme() and theme_minimal()?


ggplot(data = penguins) +
  geom_point(aes(x = body_mass_g, 
                 y = bill_length_mm,
                 color = species),
             size = 3,
             alpha = .5) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2") +
  xlab("Body Mass (g)") +
  ylab("Bill Length (mm)") +
  theme(axis.title.x = element_text(size = 14),
        axis.title.y = element_text(size = 14),
        axis.text = element_text(size = 12)) +
  theme_minimal() 

Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).









All of our modifications are lost. This is because the order of our theme matters. Because we call theme_minimal() after theme(), we overwrite any changes we made with the defaults from theme_minimal(). If we want to modify how theme_minimal() looks we do it after calling theme_minimal().


Creating Titles (and Subtitles)

Now let’s complete our plot with a title and subtitle:

ggplot(data = penguins) +
  geom_point(aes(x = body_mass_g, 
                 y = bill_length_mm,
                 color = species),
             size = 3,
             alpha = .5) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2") +
  xlab("Body Mass (g)") +
  ylab("Bill Length (mm)") +
  labs(title = "Bill length and body mass",
       subtitle = "Measurements for Adelie, Chinstrap and Gentoo Penguins at Palmer Station LTER") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.title.x = element_text(size = 14),
        axis.title.y = element_text(size = 14),
        axis.text = element_text(size = 12)) 

Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).











Modifying Legends

Now lets cleanup our legend. The species are made clear in the subtitle, so we can remove the legend title.

ggplot(data = penguins) +
  geom_point(aes(x = body_mass_g, 
                 y = bill_length_mm,
                 color = species),
             size = 3,
             alpha = .5) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2") +
  xlab("Body Mass (g)") +
  ylab("Bill Length (mm)") +
  labs(title = "Bill length and body mass",
       subtitle = "Measurements for Adelie, Chinstrap and Gentoo Penguins at Palmer Station LTER") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.title.x = element_text(size = 14),
        axis.title.y = element_text(size = 14),
        axis.text = element_text(size = 12),
        legend.title = element_blank(),
        legend.text = element_text(size = 12),
        legend.position = "bottom") 

Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).











Saving Visualizations

Now that we have a publication ready visualization, let’s save it using the ggsave function. ggsave makes saving our visualizations easier than using dev.off().

ggplot(data = penguins) +
  geom_point(aes(x = body_mass_g, 
                 y = bill_length_mm,
                 color = species),
             size = 3,
             alpha = .5) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2") +
  xlab("Body Mass (g)") +
  ylab("Bill Length (mm)") +
  labs(title = "Bill length and body mass",
       subtitle = "Measurements for Adelie, Chinstrap and Gentoo Penguins at Palmer Station LTER") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.title.x = element_text(size = 14),
        axis.title.y = element_text(size = 14),
        axis.text = element_text(size = 12),
        legend.title = element_blank(),
        legend.text = element_text(size = 12),
        legend.position = "bottom") 

Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).








ggsave("ggplot_penguins_mass_comparison.pdf",
       height = 5,
       width = 8)

Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).




Adding Statistical Summaries

Now let’s add a statistical summary. we will use the geom_smooth() layer to add fit a smoothed line to each group, along with confidence intervals. The default method (when there is less than 1000 points) is locally estimated scatterplot smoothing (LOESS) which is a generalization of a moving average and polynomial regression.

ggplot(data = penguins) +
  geom_point(aes(x = body_mass_g, 
                 y = bill_length_mm,
                 color = species),
             size = 3,
             alpha = .5) +
  geom_smooth(aes(x = body_mass_g,
                  y = bill_length_mm,
                  color = species)) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2") +
  xlab("Body Mass (g)") +
  ylab("Bill Length (mm)") +
  labs(title = "Bill length and body mass",
       subtitle = "Measurements for Adelie, Chinstrap and Gentoo Penguins at Palmer Station LTER") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.title.x = element_text(size = 14),
        axis.title.y = element_text(size = 14),
        axis.text = element_text(size = 12),
        legend.title = element_blank(),
        legend.text = element_text(size = 12),
        legend.position = "bottom") 

`geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'


Warning: Removed 2 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_smooth()`).


Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).











Local vs Global Aesthetics

Do you notice how we had to repeat the aes() arguments for geom_point() and geom_smooth()? This seems redundant. Right now we declare the aesthetics every time we add a new geom layer, but if the aesthetics are the same for every layer, we can just declare them once. Aesthetics declared in ggplot() are global, and apply to every layer. Aesthetics applied to a specific geom layer, such as geom_point() are local, and only apply to that geom layer.

ggplot(data = penguins,
       aes(x = body_mass_g,
           y = bill_length_mm,
           color = species)) +
  geom_point(size = 3,
             alpha = .5) +
  geom_smooth() +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2") +
  xlab("Body Mass (g)") +
  ylab("Bill Length (mm)") +
  labs(title = "Bill length and body mass",
       subtitle = "Measurements for Adelie, Chinstrap and Gentoo Penguins at Palmer Station LTER") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.title.x = element_text(size = 14),
        axis.title.y = element_text(size = 14),
        axis.text = element_text(size = 12),
        legend.title = element_blank(),
        legend.text = element_text(size = 12),
        legend.position = "bottom") 

`geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'


Warning: Removed 2 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_smooth()`).


Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).











Facets

If our data is too dense, we can split it into small multiples using facets:

ggplot(data = penguins,
       aes(x = flipper_length_mm,
           y = bill_length_mm,
           color = species)) +
  geom_point(size = 3,
             alpha = .5) +
  geom_smooth() +
  facet_wrap(~species, nrow = 3) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2") +
  xlab("Flipper Length (mm)") +
  ylab("Bill Length (mm)") +
  labs(title = "Bill length and body mass",
       subtitle = "Measurements for Adelie, Chinstrap and Gentoo Penguins at Palmer Station LTER") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.title.x = element_text(size = 14),
        axis.title.y = element_text(size = 14),
        axis.text = element_text(size = 12),
        legend.title = element_blank(),
        legend.text = element_text(size = 12),
        legend.position = "none") 

`geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'


Warning: Removed 2 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_smooth()`).


Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).
